There is something fundamentally jarring about the way Boots (Netflix, 2025) handles the relationship between its two young male leads, Ray and Cope. Initially, their premise was compelling: childhood best friends, bound by shared secrets, entering a Marine training camp together.
​Yet, all that potential collapses as the script feels compelled to repeatedly affirm Ray's absolute straightness, as if this were a truth that had to be hammered home across every single episode.
​The Boots writers seem obsessed with asserting the message: "Yes, straight guys can be supportive allies to their gay friends." The problem isn't the message itself, but the aggressively forced delivery. This obsession is so strong that a new female character is introduced seemingly for the sole purpose of confirming Ray's orientation. She contributes nothing meaningful to the plot, drives no character development, and adds zero depth to the story. She exists purely as an evidentiary prop.
​And at that precise moment, Boots ceases to be a human story and transforms into a social campaign thinly disguised as a teen drama.
​All the emotional richness and potential intimacy between Ray and Cope—especially within the pressure-cooker environment of a Marine camp, rife with physical and psychological tension—is aggressively skirted. Had their connection been allowed to evolve into something more profound, whether as deeply understanding friends or even a romantic couple, the series could have opened itself up to far more complex explorations of identity, closeness, and the blurred lines between fraternal and romantic love.
​But Boots chooses the safe route. It is paralyzed by fear: the fear of misinterpretation, the fear of criticism, the fear that two young men who deeply understand each other might be perceived as "more than friends."
​And therein lies the greatest irony: Boots strives for representation, but in doing so, it strips away its own humanity.
​As a viewer accustomed to Asian dramas that bravely operate in the gray zones—where ambiguity is often the central narrative strength—I found Boots utterly rigid. The series offers no breathing room for the feelings that defy easy definition, for the silent emotions that blossom in extreme situations like military training. Everything must be neat, and everything must have a label.
​Ray must be straight. Cope must be gay. Each must be assigned an appropriate "partner."
​The result? A narrative that has lost its pulse.
​I wasn't looking for explicit romance; I was looking for honesty. Because sometimes, the most resonant human connections are born between two people who simply don't know how to define what they feel. But Boots refuses to live in that space. It prefers to ensure that everything is "safe."
​It's too platonic, too stiff, and too intent on proving a point that didn't need proving in the first place.
​Ultimately, Boots isn't about Ray and Cope. It's about a Hollywood system still scrambling to reassure itself that diversity can be managed by formula.

This show was fantastic and told a compelling story of several unique types and personalities that are the makeup of our military. Yes, the overall theme and main character is about the gay character(s) within the military, especially before Clinton's "Don't Ask, Don't Tell", but the overarching theme for all the characters is: Change and Overcoming Failure. The characters of Ray, Ochoa, Santos, Nash, the Bowman's, even getting to know the DI's, all amazing arcs (loved DI McKinnon and DI Howitt). DI Sullivans plot arc was a whiplash, you felt his underlying tension rising up until the reveal of his past and it spilling over into the breakdowns we see slowly building. Whole experience was an emotional rollercoaster to belly bursting laughter at shenanigans of the recruits to the drama of consequence. 9.5/10

Attention Netflix:  I read that there were three military veteran advisors involved in the production of this show.  You should never hire them for anything again, because they got so much wrong throughout the entire show, whether the period related to 1979 (as in the book and they forgot to adjust) or 1990, as it was adapted for the series.

-Recruits were never picked up from the bus by their training platoon DIs.  They always went into a receiving platoon for about a week, and that's where they got uniforms and gear.
-Recruits never ate meals at the chow hall in civilian attire.  They were given box meals (sandwiches, fruit, hardboiled egg) before getting issued uniforms.
-After receiving platoon got all of their intake paperwork complete, they get assigned to their training platoons.

-There was no script for a phone call telling families not to send large packages or food.  They just tell families they arrived safely and will be sending a post card soon.  When they get to training platoon, recruits get post cards to mail home with their platoon mailing address, and that's when they tell not to send large packages or food or other prohibited items (contraband).  Recruits were required to open packages in front of DIs to make sure there was nothing unauthorized in the package.

-DIs never threw mail on the squad bay floor.  Mail was always handed directly to a recruit, who had to take it with both hands and acknowledge that they received their mail, out loud. 

-Recruits always wore covers when wearing their blouse outdoors.

-Recruits did not pick their own racks or bunkies.  Racks were assigned alphabetically, by last name.

-No squad leader was ever also the scribe.  The only time a recruit could be given double duty was during mess-maintenance week, where a scribe could also be a squad leader or even a guide, because the platoon was dramatically reduced in size due to mess and maintenance quotas.

-There were never female company commanders for male recruit training in the 1990s.  The females were all part of 4th Battalion at Parris Island; always segregated.
-The Company Commander would not have that much direct involvement with the platoons.  Recruits saw the series commander (a first lieutenant) far more frequently (but still not as much as the Company CO was shown in this series).  And there was a Company 1stSgt working with the Company CO and a Series Gunnery Sergeant with the Series Commander.

-DIs did not swear as shown in the series, only very rarely and never so loud that anyone but one other person could hear it.

-Inspections were NEVER conducted by someone in a different uniform than the recruits were wearing.  If recruits are in cammies, the DIs and inspector are in cammies. If recruits are in Service "A" then the DIs and inspector are in Service "A" too, etc.

-Recruits at Parris Island wore green on green PT gear.

-The Crucible did not become part of Marine Corps boot camp until the late 1996, NOT before Desert Storm.  There was no EGA ceremony in 1990.  The big thing recruits did in 1990, before graduation, was the "Mote Run" (Motivational Run), which was a fun run all over the base, not just restricted to their battalion training areas.

-The DIs did NOT conduct marksmanship training - that was all done by PMI (Primary Marksmanship Instructor; an NCO who is not a DI), and the range had several range coaches, usually one per 4 shooters (in the '90s).

-The rifleman's creed was taught as soon as rifles were issued, not at the range.

-The DIs always demonstrated every obstacle on the O-course and confidence course.

-In the 1990s, recruits did LINE training, Linear Involuntary Neural-override Engagement.  It wasn't just "punch and block" with a buddy.

-Recruit classes in auditoriums or classrooms were conducted by personnel other than their platoon DIs.   Platoon DIs only conducted classes in the squadbay.

-Mess and Maintenance week - recruits worked in the chow hall, but they were not unsupervised - there were Marine cooks and other permanent personnel at every area, supervising recruits.
-The scullery in the chow hall is a super loud environment, and trays are always emptied of food before being sent in.

-Recruits dropped to MRP (Medical Rehabilitation Platoon) or PCP (Physical Conditioning Platoon) NEVER EVER went back to their original platoon.  Once they were cleared, they would return to a new platoon that is about to reach the point of training at which they left their original platoon.  For example, if a recruit got injured in the 9th week and needed a month to recover, they would be at MRP for a month, plus however long it takes for another platoon to reach week 9, and then they would drop into that platoon.  But PCP was for people who were fat right off the bus, and they could be in PCP for a long time before meeting standards to get assigned to a training platoon.

-DIs do not call cadence from the front of the platoon, they run along side the platoon, by the middle/rear.  Why?  Because nobody would hear them in the back.

-DIs wore all the same gear recruits wore on hikes, including the ALICE pack - they did not do the hike in just cammies and campaign covers.

-Sullivan would have been required to reenlist before transferring to DI School to ensure he had enough time on his contract to accept orders and complete a tour as a DI.  He would not have been issued orders if he didn't already reenlist. And nobody got assigned straight to a platoon from the fleet.

-The Company Commander would not have been that involved in the reenlistment process, even though it was already wrong.  

-Sullivan would not have had a Silver Star or Combat Action Ribbon in 1990.  He was too young to be a Vietnam veteran, and if he was stationed in Guam, he would not have been sent to Panama between 1988-1990, as that operation only involved Marines from Camp Pendleton CA, Camp Lejeune NC and a FAST Platoon from Norfolk, Virginia.  He did not have an Armed Forces Expeditionary Medal or Purple Heart, so he was also not at Beruit in 1983.  Also, a Navy Commendation Medal as a Sgt was extremely rare in 1990 and still rare in 2025.

-In 1990, it was called NIS, not NCIS.  It was called Naval Investigative Service until 1993.

-Recruits released from medical were never sent back to their platoon in the middle of the night.

-Recruits never did IT at their racks unless the entire platoon was being punished for something.  All IT in the squad bay was done on the quarterdeck.

The Netflix series Boots is inspired by the real-life experiences of a gay Marine, specifically Greg Cope White's memoir, The Pink Marine. The show focuses on a gay teenager who goes to Marine boot camp at a time when being gay in the military was illegal, before the "Don't Ask, Don't Tell" policy was in place. While the main character, Cameron Cope, is based on Cope White, some plot points and characters, such as the drill instructor Sullivan, were created for the show but are inspired by the real stories of military members who had to hide their homosexuality.
